healthy knee: The image displays a clear and wide tibiofemoral joint space, indicating healthy cartilage thickness.
Articular surfaces of the femoral condyles and tibial plateau are smooth and well-defined.
There is a complete absence of osteophyte formation at the joint margins.
The tibial spines (intercondylar eminence) are sharp and pointed.
No evidence of subchondral sclerosis (increased bone density) is visible beneath the articular surfaces.
The subchondral bone appears uniform in density and structure.
This is a photo of a Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) Grade 0 knee, representing no radiographic signs of osteoarthritis.
The medial and lateral joint compartments show symmetric and well-preserved joint space.
The patellofemoral joint space is also clear and unobstructed.
There are no subchondral cysts (geodes) present in the bone.
The femoral and tibial bone ends show normal, rounded contours without flattening or deformity.
No signs of bony remodeling or attrition are visible.
The alignment of the femur and tibia is normal, without varus or valgus deformity.
This X-ray confirms the absence of any pathological findings related to osteoarthritis.
The joint margins are smooth, without any lipping or sharpening.
doubtful osteoarthritis: The image shows a minute, doubtful osteophyte, often seen at the medial tibial spine.
This photo represents a Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) Grade 1 knee.
The joint space width is still considered normal and well-preserved.
There is questionable or very slight joint space narrowing, but it is not definite.
Articular surfaces remain smooth, with no clear irregularities.
This image displays possible sharpening of the tibial spines, which is an equivocal sign.
No definite subchondral sclerosis is visible.
A tiny osteophytic lipping may be visible on the femoral condyles or tibial plateau.
The radiographic findings for osteoarthritis are equivocal or uncertain.
This is the earliest stage, showing only minimal, questionable changes.
The overall joint structure and alignment are maintained.
The bone contours are still normal, without significant remodeling.
This photo shows a joint that is radiographically normal, apart from a single, tiny, doubtful osteophyte.
The medial and lateral compartments do not show asymmetric narrowing.
minimal osteoarthritis: The image shows a definite, small osteophyte at the joint margins.
This photo represents a Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) Grade 2 knee, indicating mild osteoarthritis.
There is possible or minimal joint space narrowing (JSN), often in the medial compartment.
The joint space is still relatively well-preserved, but JSN may be suspected.
Definite osteophytes are clearly visible on the femoral condyles or tibial plateau.
This image may show slight subchondral sclerosis (increased bone density) starting to appear.
The tibial spines appear sharpened or more pointed due to early osteophyte formation.
No significant bony deformity is present at this stage.
The articular surfaces are mostly smooth but may show minor irregularities.
This is the first stage where osteoarthritis is definitively diagnosed on X-ray.
The bone ends are still largely normal in shape.
The alignment of the knee joint is generally maintained.
The radiographic features are mild but clear.
This photo shows small bony spurs at the edges of the joint.
moderate osteoarthritis: The image displays moderate, multiple, and definite osteophytes at the joint margins.
A key feature is moderate and definitive joint space narrowing (JSN), which is clearly visible.
This photo represents a Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) Grade 3 knee.
Moderate subchondral sclerosis is evident, appearing as increased radiodensity (whiteness) of the bone.
There is some deformity and remodeling of the bone ends, such as squaring of the femoral condyles.
This image shows significant narrowing, particularly in the medial tibiofemoral compartment.
The tibial spines are blunted, broad, and show osteophyte formation.
Small subchondral cysts (geodes) may be present, appearing as lucent areas in the bone.
The articular surfaces are becoming irregular and flattened.
This is a photo of clearly established, moderate osteoarthritis.
The joint space is clearly reduced compared to a healthy knee.
Multiple osteophytes are visible on the tibia, femur, and potentially the patella.
The bone contours are beginning to lose their normal smooth, rounded shape.
There may be early signs of varus or valgus malalignment.
The X-ray shows significant structural changes beyond just small osteophytes.
severe osteoarthritis: The image shows severe joint space narrowing, often with complete obliteration (bone-on-bone contact).
This photo represents a Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) Grade 4 knee, or end-stage osteoarthritis.
Large, numerous osteophytes are visible, causing gross deformity of the joint.
Significant and dense subchondral sclerosis is present, appearing as very dense white areas in the bone.
Large and multiple subchondral cysts are clearly visible within the femoral or tibial bone.
There is severe deformity and bony remodeling of the articular ends.
This image displays flattening and eburnation (polished appearance) of the articular surfaces.
The joint space is markedly reduced or absent, especially in one compartment.
This photo shows significant malalignment, such as a varus (bow-legged) or valgus (knock-kneed) deformity.
The tibial spines are flattened, broad, and heavily affected by osteophytes.
Bony attrition (wearing away of bone) is often visible on the articular surfaces.
Large cystic areas (geodes) are prominent in the subchondral regions.
The joint margins are grossly irregular and distorted by large bony spurs.
Loose bodies (fragments of bone or cartilage) may be visible within the joint.
This is an image of advanced structural joint failure.
